segments.text: All of the money that goes into this is obviously spent in other
agencies rather than in, uh, the
Center. Uh, everyone in this room is
acutely aware of the horrors and harm caused by family violence and sexual
violence in our country. It includes high-profile tragedies as
well as the tens of thousands of New Zealanders whose names we don't always learn,
who experience violence on a regular basis, usually behind closed
doors. When someone makes the difficult decision to leave a violent relationship
or get help in the aftermath of sexual assault, they need a safe and
appropriate response from trained professionals who understand the complexities of
family violence and sexual violence, and who can work with them to provide support
and make a safety plan. Family and sexual violence are some of the
most entrenched problems we face.
They can't be solved by any one government or government agency, but
we can make traction if we work together.
Uh, Te Orirekura is a 25-year strategy for intergenerational
change, and the executive board is committed to building the necessary
foundations to realize that shared vision.
Uh, during 24-25, the executive board agreed core operating procedures
to try and sharpen up each agency's responsibilities, including
expectations about information sharing.
Uh, the executive board, um, uh, set,
presented new strategic intentions to the House, and they set out where we will
focus our efforts in the medium term, including system stewardship,
evidence-based investment, and better outcomes for people affected by
violence. So, I will, uh, a- almost finish my
opening remarks and hand over to Emma.
I would just, in closing, say family violence and sexual violence are one of the
main drivers of violent crime in New Zealand.
The data shows that a significant percentage of victims have experienced multiple
victimizations. Family violence is a pattern of behavior, and the cycle
is difficult to break entirely. By working together and
sustaining our focus, the executive board can use a variety of levers across a
range of key agencies to improve crisis response, build prevention
system

often, if not forgotten altogether.
Can I illustrate it (clears throat) this way?
This year began badly. On the 1st of January, a
policewoman was run over and killed in Nelson.
Later that same day, a child was beaten and stabbed to death in
Hamilton. Hands up those who remember the death of the
policewoman? Now hands up those who remember the
child? Why do we forget the
children? As far as I'm concerned, I've seen no
effect of the center out there at all
in the people that I work with around New Zealand.
I learned a life lesson while doing some work in
(foreign language) from a 10-year-old boy.
The children were discussing their new program with parents saying what they
could and couldn't do. Frustrated, little Riri
said, "Adults need to listen louder."
Have you been listening loud enough to me?
I had a daughter.
(cries) I don't want you to lose yours.
How hard are you going to push to prevent the next
Helen from being murdered? Do you have any
questions?
Well, thank you, Mr. White, and, um, it's obviously difficult to
deliver, so I appreciate you coming here.
Um, we don't have a lot of time, but I'm going to ask Mr.
Rutherford.
Yeah. Thank you, Mr. White. Thank you for your advocacy in making the effort
to come and see us today as well. Um, one of the things that you
note in your petition is that we as a
country are really good at writing reports and
holding meetings. These are words that you've used.
But they never get followed through enough.
Most get briefly read and justifiably forgotten.
You've given the example of a million dollars and a five-year result, say
in Northland. Could you talk us through what you would do in
that five years with that million dollars that you referenced?
'Cause it would be great to hear what you could achieve on the ground with
something like that.
Well, if you look at what we did in Huntly and what was done in Ohakune and other
places, the very first step that was done s- was
to go to the community and ask
them. And I mean you knock on every door.
Um, it was a process that was done in Mowera 40 years ago.
You ask the families two simple questions: "What can we do
to change your life in this community to make it better?" And, "What are
your skills to help do it?" Because we found
people who'd shifted to Ohakune that was a midwife and they didn't have
a midwife, because her husband had gone there for an engineering
job. We found people
that had a, a myriad of ideas and successes.
We had, um, a guy who worked at NASA who'd retired in
Ohakune and he brought all of NASA's computers into Ohakune for the kids
'cause they didn't have any. Ask people what they contribute, but
th- most importantly when you talk to a family in their house and
say, "What can we do to make your life better in this particular town?"
they will tell you. And the glory of it is that if you're sitting in a Maori
family, you are getting everything of it from a Maori point of
view. If you're talking in a Chinese family, you get those perspective of
what is important culturally to them, and you add that into what you
do. And, uh, and when we did Ohakune, um, when
they put all those questionnaires back together and they sat down,
they put it all into what they called their scoping report, which allowed the
group of locals who were able then to
decide how they were going to go about what they were doing, and they ended up with
five headings to make it easier to work with.
And you'll guess the five: housing, health, education,
employment, and social issues....
and they set their targets, and they set a five-year period and they achieved a
lot. And to me, the most remarkable one was
the, the pass rate at the school where they had a 50%
failure rate at NZ, you know, the level two.
Mm-hmm.
That went up to 92.9 in four years
because people finally understood what their kids could do
and what their life would be if they had level two
without... The social side is getting people together and, and
there's a whole myriad of, of projects they did.
Um,
last, um, last year they brought in a new one in the
competition, um, and they brought Euchre into it.
How many people here play Euchre?
I played.
Yeah. So you had to teach a whole town how to play Euchre, which meant-
(laughs)
... you had to get people sitting down talking that never before sat down
and talked together, and that is the crux of it.
You have to have communication and you have to have the local people
talking so that you can solve problems.
Remarkably, Ohakune family and sexual
violence wasn't one of the 32 f- first projects,
but they reduced it by 40%-
Mm-hmm.
... because they got rid of the problems.
Okay, I've got last question from, uh, Ms. White.
Thank you, um, thank you for coming and talking through this.
I'm not usually on this committee, but I have this portfolio for
Labor. Um, I want to ask you about the
suicide rates and the connection, and why you think
it would make a big difference if we adopted a policy
of taking into account and investigating those
suicides.
Well, uh,
at the moment, our family violence
deaths, murders, sit around the 30.
If you talk to any group that work with suicidal people
and you get the analysis of what they're doing, keeping in mind we've got over 600
suicides a year, they all say at least half of
people considering suicide have been sexually
abused, or it comes from family violence.
They would.
And, and, and so you take that and put, make it into one lump sum
of, of putting those deaths together, you are forced to look at a far,
far bigger death rate than you are presently looking, and that would shift it
up into a major crime and it needs to get that attention.
And they do inquests so they're much more aware?
Yeah.
Thank you.
Thank you, Mr. White. Um, been very helpful.
Uh, we've gone probably nearly double your time, so um-
That's all right, I'm making up-
that's good. ... for the other submissions you didn't hear.
Yeah.
Mm-hmm.
Thank you very much. Now we're going to hear, uh, we're going to do a bit of a
swap. We're going to hear from the, the, um-
Sorry, Mr. Chair, just to address your criticism, it's
not that we didn't... It's this is, this is our usual process to hear from the
petitioner first before we decide what to do next.
So it's still, we're still open to...
Yeah.
That might be-
What, what may happen next.
Yeah, so sorry, I just-
Okay.
... don't want you leaving the impression...
Most committees, when they hear a petition, wouldn't open submissions immediately.
They want to hear from the petitioner first.
Well, I apologize because-
No, sorry, it's fine.
... I actually asked this committee
at a point, "Why aren't there other submissions?" And I was told,
"Because they've decided not to."
Well, at this stage, at this stage they've not.
At this stage.
Yeah, at this stage. So, yeah.
Okay, so-
Hopeful.
So that was... Yeah.
Mm-hmm.
Right, let's have the, um, center back out, thanks.
Thank you, Mr., uh,
White.
Anybody
Insistent.
All right, yes, please.
Uh, thank you, Chair and, uh, committee members again for the opportunity for us
to talk in this, and, uh, thank you, Mr. White.
I'd like to start by acknowledging Mr.
White's tireless advocacy for improvements to the family violence
system. We are, we are always better in for efforts of
people like Mr. White and dependent on them.
I acknowledge the tragic loss
of your daughter, Eloise, and I thank, I thank
Mr. White for generously sharing his experiences in order to make
change and prevent family violence in our communities.
I'll ask Emma to respond to Mr. White's petition,
uh, in a more detailed way.
Emma?
Um, thank you, Andrew, and thanks to the chair and the committee for the
chance to both be here present to hear Mr.
White, um, and to have that opportunity to respond.
Um, I was fortunate enough actually to travel to David's home
earlier this year in March and hear firsthand his
frustrations, um, and ideas for the future, um, and really,
really welcomed those. Um, and I just wanted to continue to extend
that outreach and opportunity to continue the dialogue beyond
today. Um, Mr. White
raises some really critical and fundamental issues for which we
wholeheartedly agree. Um, we would all hope for and wish for
faster, more urgent progress, and in particular, a greater
focus on primary prevention. Mr. White talks
of, uh, community mobilization on a scale and level
which, uh, you know, really aims to, uh,
improve and uphold protective factors and cohesion in our
communities, which done well and together, uh, seeks to
disarm and, uh, eradicate violence.
Um, we do have several primary prevention and
prevention programs, uh, that a range of different agencies
fund and deliver. E Tū Whānau, Pasifika Proud, we've
had the It's Not OK campaign, um, although I know that that
has probably not been as prevalent and, and, and felt by New
Zealanders in the last sort of few years.
Um-We, too, agree and is does
(clicks tongue) Te Aorerekura sets out the need for
strengths-based, um, approaches and community mobilization.
Those are the two key shifts, uh, in Te Aorerekura that speaks to
that. Uh, what, what we can commit to is
ensuring that we are promoting much more community-led
responses, um, and supports, um, in how we seek to enable that
through sort of community grants and support.
Um, the issue of, uh, suicides,
uh, and the drivers of suicide being really closely
connected to all forms of trauma, um, including sexual violence and
family violence, um, are, are really important.
Um, the most recent Family Violence Death Review Committee, Mortality
Review Committee into femicide certainly started to make those links
between maternal mental health, um, and, and
suicide. And as the center, we are continuing to commit
to work in partnership with the Mortality Review Committee and HQSC
and other, um, places to start to look at what can be done to
understand the connection and greater visibility, um, of, of
suicide, um, linked to family violence.
Uh, we do know for some of those, uh, young mothers, um,
that, uh, for about 60% of them, family violence was part
of that, that history. So, um, Mr. White is absolutely
right in, in raising that with the committee.
Um, I'm choosing not to sit here and defend the center
necessarily, in terms of, um, our value.
I think one of the challenges we've always had is understanding the role which
we play, which is often one of enablement as opposed
to, uh, direct doing in community.
Um, our role needs to continue to work with urgency to
ensure that what we do translates into
actionable traction and change on the ground and
impact to victims, whānau, um, and
community. Um, I'll leave it there.
I just, really grateful for the opportunity and, um, whilst I'm not looking at you,
David, um, (laughs) I'm greatly, I'm, I'm hugely thankful for your,
um, courage and commitment, um, and your, your continued
advocacy. Um, and I'm sorry we haven't done
more and faster.
Right. I've got a couple of questions, uh, probably th- three.
Uh, Mr. Radford?
Yeah. Thank, thank you, Emma. Why is family and sexual violence so prevalent in
New Zealand?
(laughs) Right. (laughs)
there are a range of historical and current state
issues that really combine to create, um, violence in this
country. Um, uh-
I think one of your colleagues, sorry, has a, something behind you.
Oh, thank you. Um, in terms of the things that
drive, um, and risk factors for, uh, family
violence, um, and sexual violence, um, there are a range of things.
And I think that's why it's often quite challenging to, just to point to the one
thing to, to solve for. Um, harmful drug and alcohol use,
um, and, uh, untreated, uh, mental health, uh, challenges
are definitely one of those. Institutional and systemic
bias, racism, ageism, sexism, uh,
misogyny, uh, colonization, a whole range of
systemic long-term entrenched factors that create the
conditions for violence to thrive.
Um, poor system responses. Quite often the system
can, can harm, um, and create harm just as a
perpetrator might create harm
not having the right social and economic supports, um, available to
people to live well and thriving lives.
Um, and also a lack of supports for people, carers, whānau and vulnerable
people. So, those are the things that our strategy sets out as the
major drivers of violence. Um,
inter- you know, they are intergenerational, they are, uh,
systemic by their very nature and therefore require a, an
effort on all fronts.
Okay, thank you.
Right. Uh, Mr. Sanderson?
Thank you. Um,
uh, Mr. White, in addition to presenting to us (crosstalk) has given us a really
excellent-
Yeah.
Yeah. ... written submission as well. I'm sure you've seen access to that.
We've received that, yeah.
And I think, um, look, I think we talked much earlier in our review
about coordination, um, and hopefully he, one of his other ideas which
he actually references a social investment agency-
Yes. Yeah.
... which you are working with. So maybe again that will give him some comfort.
But what, another idea which I think seems like a very simple one is,
um, Life Education Trust.
Mm-hmm.
So again, uh, he very much focused on prevention, which I think obviously
super important. Um, yeah. Is there, are you working, I
suppose, almost at that school and, and early education level to
try and, yeah, get some of these, um, themes
into, yeah-
Yeah.
... our young people?
Y- yeah. Yeah. Absolutely. And I think when we start to talk about prevention and
primary prevention more universally, it needs to show up in all
of those settings, um, in different places.
So education settings, a- absolutely.
Um,
one of the things that we are doing to b- to bring visibility...
So we talked earlier today about a baseline review into the direct spend
around family violence and sexual violence in all of those services.
One of the next phases of that is to look at all of those
indirect supports, services, initiatives
that are exactly like Life Education Trust, exactly like other
broader parenting programs and supports maternal mental health, et cetera,
to start to get a picture of those other, um, what we call
indirect spend, but that, those other supports in communities and that government
is, is funding to try to get a much broader picture about how could
we help to orientate those toward violence prevention by their very
nature? Um, there are several, uh, early
education, uh, you know, supports and programs certainly in
terms of, uh, child protection training and support there.
There are, um, in pockets, programs around sexual, um, abuse
prevention as well. Again, some of these are not widely
scaled across the country, but there are places where it is dedicated to young
people.
Okay. Good questions.
Thank you. So with the new, um, Family Violence Sexual Violence
Ministerial Advisory Group-
Mm-hmm.
... do you think that this is something that, um, you would see them looking
into?
So, um, urgently establishing a nationwide family violence and
sexual violence prevention program and, uh-...
include death by suicide when linked to family violence in official
stats. Is that something you see as could be part of their work plan?
Um, it, it could be. Um, uh, that group is a
ministerial advisory group.
So is appointed and reports through direct to the minister.
Um, the center will, uh, be the secretariat support and
provide support and guidance to that.
So, I think that's a really good, um, forum to potentially
take this. I think having, um, particularly today, Andrew
and I being part of this process-
Mm-hmm.
... is something for us to take back to the rest of the IEB and board, in
particular the two issues raised through the petition around the Adaptive
Prevention Programme and, uh, the, the suicide connection with family
violence and sexual violence as a matter of absolute course, um,
immediately. Um, but that certainly could be something that future
advisors look at.
Um, I, I'm going to ask a question. Um, if we distill down
Mr. White's petition, it has two key legs.
First one is to focus on prevention.
Mm-hmm.
And, uh, I think there may be some confusion about
your role as a enabler, uh, as opposed
to someone who
is an agency that delivers, because that's spread across
everywhere, and it's probably a lack of transparency.
So you talked, uh, Ms. Kielbreht, about the figure north of 700
million. So I think having that visibility around the prevention and the
effectiveness of that is really crucial because I
think that may or may not address Mr.
White's issue around there's not sufficient investment in that
prevention. That's the first point.
The second point you raised was around, uh, the statistics,
the accounting for suicides and why we don't
reflect, um, those, a lot of the
suicides that are related to sexual
violence and family violence. So, we haven't addressed that bit and I'm quite
keen just to hear your reflections on that.
Yeah. Um, I think as we set out in our submission that
there's currently no centrally recorded place for that
information. Um, and
however, that all family homicides are re-
reviewed by the Mortality Review Committee.
And that would be an important way in for us to start to think about
that. Um, that again, um,
is one route in. It is not that it wouldn't give you the whole
picture. I think we would need to take a concerted joint
effort to say that was something we were going to do and set up the
right information-gathering, um, approaches and data
collation to help us with that. Um,
a- as I say, I think, a- and as Mr. White has set out, there are some models,
particularly in Australia, um, Western Australia, I think, um, already
start to do this, um, and have a- and able to sort of show that.
So there are models that we could learn from.
So I, I would think that the committee will come to a
view, and obviously we're gonna have a discussion, but I think the committee
would urge you to look at it, and it h-
it dovetails into what the Minister of Mental Health is doing
because obviously there is a consequence to some of th- what happens in
that process. There's obviously other, um, arrangements.
So
actually having
a wider perspective on it, and again, as an
agency, you may or may not be the right agency, but at
least maybe the question should be if you're n-
if you are the right agency, how you can facilitate
greater access to greater data on that.
And if you aren't the right agency-
Mm-hmm.
... where you think that best be handled.
Mm-hmm.
And I think I would urge you to contemplate those two issues.
Mm-hmm.
Okay. I'm going to bring this to a close.
I th- again, I just wanted to acknowledge, uh, Mr.
White, um, for his contribution. Also, to you
two and your, um, department for responding
immediately. It's not always easy with the petitioner in the room, but I
think that o- makes for open democracy and then actually
it's quite good to see some accountability.
So thank you again everyone, everyone in the room, I just want to
acknowledge you on a quite a difficult topic, um, and
unfortunately something that's, um, a nasty secret of New
Zealand. And, uh, the more we bring it to light and the more we bring about
policies that deal with it, uh, we should all encourage that and bring
it into the light. Thank you very much.
Mm-hmm.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Just quickly, ??????.
Yeah, I'm gonna... Sorry, I'm gonna ask you to move because I've got, we've got the
auditor general we've gotta have a little session with, so we're gonna go into
private.
I'll second.
So thank you. Thank you, everyone. Thanks, everyone.
Thank you.
Thank you.
